food_name: Agar Jelly Three Colours (Thạch Rau Câu Ba Màu)

allergens: coconut milk (tree nut derivative — check formulation)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Vietnamese three-colour agar jelly is approximately 150 g, representing one individual dessert portion as commonly served in Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Agar, plain (cooked) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with agar jelly dessert composition estimates from FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Tables for Southeast Asia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Vietnamese three-colour agar jelly (Thạch Rau Câu Ba Màu) exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA tables. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy agar (plain, cooked) as the primary structural ingredient, adjusted for added sugar (~8–10 g/100 g), coconut milk contribution (~1–2 g fat/100 g), and pandan flavouring. The resulting composition reflects a sweetened, lightly coconut-enriched agar jelly typical of this dessert category. Protein is negligible as agar is a polysaccharide with no significant protein content.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of agar-based jelly and analogous gelatine/agar desserts — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese three-colour agar jelly (Thạch Rau Câu Ba Màu). GI is estimated at 40 based on analogous agar-based jelly desserts in the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of GI Values (Atkinson et al., 2008). Agar is a non-digestible polysaccharide that does not contribute to glycaemic response; the GI of the product is driven primarily by the added sugar content (~9 g/100 g). A GI of 40 is consistent with lightly sweetened agar jelly products. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (40 × 13.5 × 1.5) / 100 = 8.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~8 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Vietnamese three-colour agar jelly is very low in sodium (~12 mg per 150 g serving) and poses minimal concern for individuals managing hypertension; however, portion size should be monitored due to added sugar content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar content (~9 g/100 g) and moderate glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 40 (low) but a glycemic load of approximately 8 per 150 g serving driven by added sugar, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size and account for this dessert within their daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat and negligible cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Vietnamese three-colour agar jelly contains minimal saturated fat (~0.7 g/100 g from coconut milk) and negligible cholesterol, making it a low cardiovascular risk dessert when consumed in moderate portions; the primary concern is the added sugar contributing to caloric intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thạch Rau Câu Ba Màu (Vietnamese Three-Colour Agar Jelly) is a traditional Vietnamese dessert made from agar-agar powder dissolved in water and sweetened with sugar, then set into firm, translucent jelly layers. The three distinct coloured layers are typically achieved using natural or food-grade colourings such as pandan extract (green), coconut milk (white or cream), and fruit-based or floral extracts (pink or red), giving the dessert its characteristic visual appeal. The jelly is prepared by boiling agar with water and sugar, pouring each layer sequentially and allowing each to set before adding the next, then chilling the assembled dessert until fully firm. The texture is smooth, firm, and slightly springy — characteristic of agar-based jellies — with a mildly sweet flavour and subtle pandan or coconut aroma depending on the layer. Agar-agar is a plant-derived polysaccharide extracted from red algae, making this dessert naturally vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free (when coconut milk is used instead of dairy). Nutritionally, the dessert is low in protein and fat, with energy derived primarily from added sugar and the agar polysaccharide. The estimated glycemic index is 40 (low), though the glycemic load per serving (~8) is moderate due to the sugar content, making portion control relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is very low, posing minimal concern for hypertension. The dessert is popular across Vietnam and in Vietnamese diaspora communities in Singapore and Southeast Asia, often served chilled as a refreshing end to a meal or as a standalone snack.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Vietnamese three-colour agar jelly was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA tables. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy agar (plain, cooked) adjusted for added sugar and coconut milk contributions, consistent with the known composition of this dessert category. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for agar-based jelly desserts. Confidence is medium rather than low because the ingredient composition of this dish is well-characterised and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
